alternatives.transcript: Alright. Let's mix it up. Let's go check out a different repo. I was talking a lot about Clanker Maker, but let's talk DEFCON run 34. DEFCON run 34 is kind of the original AWS infrastructure project. It's it's got a bit of a background, a bit of legacy to it because you can see there's a DEFCON run 33, 32, 31, years of me kind of refining this idea. I think I think as they say as as they say, we have arrived, we've arrived. DEFCON run 34 is kind of the final implementation. If you're gonna do a TeraGrunt module for multi region on top of Terraform, this is kind of the way I would I would do it. It's the way I've done it. And if you check out DEFCON run 34, there's a there's an infra folder and inside and inside that infra folder, you can see that you can see the structure that's at play. And and the most powerful part about this structure and the thing that gets me that usually gets me really excited about DEFCON RUN when I start talking about it is is the multi region solve. The the fact that I've solved multi region AWS wrinkles kind of in a way that makes me feel like Vercel or 1 of those newer providers that are basically reselling. But if you've ever tried to do AWS you know that what I'll call the Global Region Gotcha. The Global Region Gotcha is the idea that even though you're using CloudFront and it's a global service, you still need to use US East 1. Even though it's a global service for the AWS WAF, you still need to say US East 1. It it doesn't make any sense but it it also kinda totally makes sense given how old and the legacy of AWS. So, of course, there are these are these wrinkles, uh, around regions and global regions and and whatever. But what's awesome about DEFCON RUN and DEFCON RUN is kind of the foundation infrastructure that I use for Clanker Maker, that I use for Clanker Voice. It's it's because as I mentioned, it is the way that I will and do multi region AWS stuff. It's just a it's a great template to start from. It's and I'm I'm proving it time and time again. So if you go into that site and you go check out the info folder, you'll see kinda how I've laid it out. So the the concept is kind of this. You you're you're either you're either thinking about the site that you have deployed, um, like, quite literally the instance of the site, and that's the
